sutta: The Buddha said: "Monks, these two struggles are hard to undergo in the world. What are the two? The struggle of householders who live at home to provide clothing, food, lodging, medicines for the sick, and the other necessities of life; and the struggle of those who have gone forth from home to homelessness to renounce all bases of rebirth. These are the two struggles. Of these two, monks, the latter is the more important. Therefore, I say to you: 'Thus must you train yourselves — we will undertake the struggle to renounce all substrates of rebirth.' That is how you must train yourselves, monks."
commentary: The Buddha is saying that the struggle of householders is to provide the necessities for the home, while the struggle of monks is to renounce the supports or bases of rebirth. The struggle of the monks is the more important one. Sometimes we find that a lot of people go through life struggling with one thing after another. When they are young, they struggle to get a good education; after that, they want a good job; then a good marriage partner; then children; then a car, a house, grandchildren, and so on. At the end of it all, when they are about to die, many people feel very confused and disappointed. They wonder what life was all about. All the things they struggled for and mostly attained — suddenly they have to lose all of it. So we keep coming back again and again to this kind of struggle. But the Buddha said that the struggle to end the cycle of rebirth is far more important.

sutta: Monks, there are these two things that burn. What are the two? Herein, a certain person has done immoral acts of body, speech, and mind. He has omitted moral acts of body, speech, and mind. He is burned and seared with remorse, thinking: 'I have done wrong in body, speech, and mind. I have left undone the good deeds in body, speech, and mind.' And he burns at the thought of it. These, monks, are the two things that burn.
commentary: The Buddha says that if we have done immoral acts (unwholesome kamma) in body, speech, and mind, or if we have omitted to do moral acts (wholesome kamma), then our conscience is pricked. Sometimes it is not only that we do something immoral — even if we fail to do something that we ought to have done, later it will burn our conscience. That is why we have to be careful. Immoral acts of the body might include killing, stealing, or sexual misconduct. Immoral acts of speech might include lying, slandering, or divisive speech. Immoral acts of thought might include ill will or hatred towards others.

sutta: The Buddha said: "Monks, there are these two things that I have realized to be conducive to progress. What are the two? Not to be discontented with good states, and not to shrink back from the struggle. Without shrinking back, monks, I struggled on, thinking: 'Gladly would I let my skin, sinews, and bones remain, and let my body's flesh and blood dry up, if only I may hold out until I win what may be won by human strength, human energy, and human striving.' By my earnest endeavor, monks, I won enlightenment; I won the unrivaled freedom from bondage. You too, monks, should not decline the fight, but struggle on, saying to yourselves: 'Gladly would I let my skin, sinews, and bones remain, and let my body's flesh and blood dry up, if only I may hold out until I win what may be won by human strength, human energy, and human striving.' Then, monks, in no long time you shall win that goal for which clansmen rightly go forth from home into homelessness — that unrivaled goal of the holy life. Realizing it for yourselves even in this very life, you shall abide therein. Therefore, I say to you: 'Thus must you train yourselves — we will not decline the fight, but will struggle on with this thought: Let skin, sinews, and bones wither away...' That is how you must train yourselves, monks."
commentary: There is a sutta in the Majjhima Nikāya (I think number 12) where the Buddha described the struggle he underwent during his six years of ascetic practice. It is quite moving to read about the various types of suffering he endured. The Buddha said that no one in the past could have suffered more than him on the spiritual path, and in the future, no one could possibly suffer more than him either — at most, it could only equal the amount of suffering he went through. Our teacher is someone who exerted himself to the utmost. That is why I also encourage us to put in a lot of effort. How much effort we put in will determine how much we get out of the spiritual path. The spiritual path is concerned with letting go — letting go of the ego, our likes and dislikes, our opinions, and anything connected with self. That is why it is very important for a meditator to always observe the mind and see the ego at work.

items: not shrinking back from the struggle

sutta: The Buddha said: "Monks, there are these two attitudes. What are the two? Looking with satisfaction on things that are fetters binding to rebirth, and looking with disgust on them. Monks, he who dwells looking with satisfaction on things that bind like fetters does not abandon greed, does not abandon hatred, does not abandon delusion. He who does not abandon these is not released from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, woe, lamentation, and despair. He is not released from dukkha, I say. But, monks, he who dwells looking with disgust on things that are fetters binding to rebirth abandons greed, hatred, and delusion. Abandoning these, he is released from old age and decay, and so on. His release from dukkha is declared."
commentary: The Buddha is saying that there are certain things — such as attachments to relatives, property, and wealth — that act as fetters binding us to the wheel of saṃsāra. To an unlearned person, these things appear delightful. He is very happy and satisfied with his big house, money in the bank, beautiful children, loving spouse, and so on. These very things that give worldly joy are the ones that bind us to rebirth. That is why it is difficult to walk the spiritual path. Most people want to enjoy these things because it is normal for worldly people to take delight in family, good music, entertainment, and so forth. But these are the very things that bring us back to rebirth. If we have the wisdom of the Dhamma, we understand that these are the bonds of saṃsāra. The worldly path and the spiritual path go in opposite directions. Some people try to get the best of both worlds, but it is very difficult. If you want to make real progress, you eventually have to renounce.

sutta: The Buddha said: "Monks, these two bright states protect the world. What are the two? Sense of shame (hiri) and fear of blame (ottappa) — which can also be translated as conscience and moral dread. Monks, if these two states did not protect the world, there would be no respect for mothers, mothers' sisters, uncles' wives, teachers' wives, or wives of honorable men. The world would come to confusion and promiscuity such as exists among goats and sheep, fowls and swine, dogs and jackals. But, monks, since these two bright states do protect the world, therefore mothers, mothers' sisters, uncles' wives, teachers' wives, and so on are respected."
commentary: The Buddha is saying that because we have a sense of conscience (shame at doing wrong) and moral dread (fear of blame or remorse), people do not behave like animals. Animals do not recognize boundaries of relationship and are highly promiscuous. Human beings generally do not act that way, though unfortunately we do see cases of incest and immoral behavior in society today. A person without shame and moral dread will keep doing wrong things until it no longer pains their conscience. If we behave like animals, it is very likely that in the next rebirth we will be born as an animal. If we behave like devas, it is likely we will be reborn in the deva realm.

sutta: The Buddha said: "Monks, if in any dispute the offending monk and the reproving monk do not practice strict self-examination, it may be expected that this will lead to protracted, bitter, contentious strife and the monks will be unable to live at ease. But when both the offending monk and the reproving monk do practice strict self-examination, it may be expected that this will not lead to protracted strife and that the monks will be able to live at ease."
commentary: This sutta is primarily for monks, but it applies equally to lay people. Whenever two parties are quarreling or there is ill feeling, we should examine ourselves honestly, see our own faults, and admit them. If we practice self-examination, we can apologize or at least avoid prolonging the dispute. (The sutta then continues with a detailed explanation of how both parties should reflect on their own contributions to the conflict, comparing it to paying duty on goods.) This sutta is about kamma again — the Buddha explains that rebirth in woeful or happy states is due to commission (doing immoral or moral acts) and omission (failing to do moral or immoral acts).

sutta: The Buddha said: "Monks, it is because I observe these two results that I am given to dwelling in lonely spots, in solitary lodging in the forest. What are the two? Observing my own pleasant abiding in this very life, and feeling compassion for future generations."
commentary: The Buddha likes to live in secluded places — forests or caves — for two reasons. First, for his own pleasant abiding: when a monk stays alone, there is no disturbance and he is free to meditate, which brings great bliss and progress on the spiritual path. Second, out of compassion for future generations: by living this way, he sets an example and encourages other monks to dwell in seclusion and cultivate themselves. Of course, not all monks are ready for solitary practice; if attempted too early, it can have a negative effect. (Note: There is a minor difference here with some Mahāyāna bodhisattva precepts that discourage staying completely alone.)

sutta: The Buddha said: "Monks, these two conditions have a part in knowledge. What are the two? Samatha (tranquility) and vipassanā (insight). When samatha is cultivated, the mind is cultivated. What is the profit of a cultivated mind? All lust is abandoned. When vipassanā is cultivated, insight is cultivated. What is the profit of cultivated insight? All ignorance is abandoned. The mind defiled by lust is not set free, nor can insight defiled by ignorance be cultivated. Indeed, monks, the abandoning of lust is the mind's release; the abandoning of ignorance is liberation by wisdom."
commentary: This is one of the important suttas on meditation. The Buddha teaches that both samatha and vipassanā are necessary for knowledge. Samatha leads to the abandonment of lust (by developing concentration and jhānas), while vipassanā leads to insight and the abandonment of ignorance. Both are required. (The commentary then discusses different paths to arahantship, liberation by mind vs. liberation by wisdom, and the necessity of both practices, with references to other suttas like AN 4.170 and AN 6.29.)

sutta: The Buddha said: "Monks, I will teach you the condition of the unworthy and the condition of the worthy. Listen closely. The unworthy person is ungrateful and forgetful of benefits. This ingratitude and forgetfulness is congenial to mean people; it is a feature of unworthy people. The worthy person is grateful and mindful of benefits done to him. This gratitude and mindfulness is congenial to the best people; it is a feature of the best people."
commentary: This teaches us to be grateful to those who have benefited us — especially our parents, elders, and teachers. A person who is ungrateful is considered mean according to the Buddha.

sutta: The Buddha said: "Monks, one can never fully repay two persons, I declare. What are the two? Mother and father. Even if one should carry one's mother on one shoulder and one's father on the other for a hundred years, supporting them by anointing, bathing, and massaging their limbs, even if they should void their excrements upon one — even so, one could not repay one's parents. Moreover, if one should establish one's parents in supreme authority and absolute rule over this mighty earth abounding in the seven treasures, not even thus could one repay them. Why is that? Because parents do much for their children: they bring them up, nourish them, and introduce them to this world. However, monks, whoever incites his unbelieving parents and establishes them in faith; incites his immoral parents and establishes them in morality; incites his stingy parents and establishes them in generosity; or incites his foolish parents and establishes them in wisdom — such a one does more than repay what is due to his parents."
commentary: Even if we care for our parents perfectly for a hundred years, we still cannot fully repay the debt we owe them for raising us, especially the care given by our mother. The best way to repay them is to help establish them in faith (in the Buddha, Dhamma, and Saṅgha), morality (sīla), generosity (dāna), and wisdom.

sutta: The householder Anāthapiṇḍika came to visit the Exalted One, saluted him, and sat down at one side. He asked: "Lord, how many in the world are worthy of offerings, and where should an offering be made?" The Buddha answered: "Two persons in the world, householder, are worthy of offerings: the learner (sekha) and the adept (asekha). These two are worthy of offerings, and to them an offering should be made."
commentary: The Buddha is referring to noble persons (ariyas) — those on the path (learners) and those who have completed the path (adepts/arahants). This does not mean that only ariyans are worthy of support. Laypeople have a duty to support the entire Saṅgha with the four requisites. Even if some monks in the future may be corrupt, supporting the Saṅgha as a whole helps perpetuate the teaching. It is generally more meritorious to offer to the Saṅgha collectively than to an individual monk.

sutta: The Buddha said: "Monks, there are these two companies. What are the two? The company trained in bluster (speaking empty, arrogant words, not in discussion and enquiry) and the company trained in discussion by enquiry, not in speaking empty arrogant words." (The sutta then contrasts the two: one company ignores the profound discourses of the Tathāgata and prefers poetic, external teachings; the other listens to the deep suttas, discusses their meaning, opens up the unrevealed, explains the unexplained, and dispels doubts.)
commentary: The Buddha encourages us to study and discuss the original discourses of the Buddha (the suttas) rather than later teachings or poetic works. He also encourages dhamma discussion (dhammasākacchā). The Buddha warned that in the future people might lose interest in the suttas, which could lead to the disappearance of the true teaching. That is why we should rely primarily on the early four Nikāyas, which are consistent and accepted by all major Buddhist traditions.

items: company trained in discussion by enquiry

sutta: The Buddha said: "Monks, there are these two fools. What are the two? He who takes up a burden that does not befall him, and he who shirks a burden that befalls him."

items: takes up a burden not his own
shirks a burden that is his

sutta: Monks, there are these two wise ones. What are the two? He who takes up a burden that befalls him, and he who does not take up one that does not befall him.
commentary: Our duty or 'burden' — for example, responsibilities toward family — should be taken up. What is not our responsibility, we should not take upon ourselves.

items: takes up the burden that is his
does not take up what is not his

sutta: Monks, these two persons are hard to find in the world. What are the two? The one who is first to do a favor, and the one who is grateful for what is done.
commentary: A person who does a kind deed first, without any prior obligation, is rare. Equally rare is a person who remains grateful even after time has passed.

items: one first to do a favor
one grateful for what is done

sutta: Monks, these two things are causes for the arising of wrong view. What are the two? Another's utterance and improper attention (ayoniso manasikāra).
Monks, these two things are causes for the arising of right view. What are the two? Another's utterance and thorough/proper attention (yoniso manasikāra).
commentary: To develop right view (which can lead to stream-entry), one listens to the teaching and then considers it with proper, thorough attention. The same process with improper attention can lead to wrong view.

sutta: The Buddha said: "The believing monk, if he would aspire perfectly, should thus aspire: 'May I be like Sāriputta and Moggallāna.' These two are the measure and standard for my monk disciples — Sāriputta and Moggallāna."
commentary: Sāriputta was foremost in wisdom and Moggallāna foremost in psychic powers. The Buddha encourages his monk disciples to aspire to be like them. This teaching contrasts with some later Mahāyāna portrayals that sometimes depict Sāriputta as having inferior understanding.
